summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the children live with the father – Where the mother abducted one of the children during a supervised visit – Where the mother received a conviction and has only had supervised phone contact with one of the children since then – Where the father seeks to relocate – Where the father and Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) seek orders for the mother to spend supervised time with the children on four occasions each year – Where the mother did not participate at the final hearing – Orders made in accordance with the proposals of the father and the ICL

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

These proceedings concern the applicant father, Mr Aberg born in 1986 (“the father”) and the respondent mother, Ms Nurimen born in 1987, relating to the parenting arrangements for the children X born in 2011 (“[X]”) and Y born in 2017 (“[Y]”) (“the children”).

BACKGROUND

The parties commenced a relationship in 2010 and separated on a final basis in mid-2017. Between December 2017 and November 2019, the children lived primarily with the mother and spent regular overnight time with the father. In late 2018, X was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder (level II), ADHD and oppositional defiant disorder.
In November 2019, the mother presented to G Hospital having self-harmed and the children remained in the father’s care.
On 22 November 2019, the father commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit Court of Australia (as it then was) and in December 2019, the children resumed living with the mother and spending time with the father.
In June 2020, the mother again presented to G Hospital having self-harmed and in July 2020 the mother presented to G Hospital following a drug overdose.
In mid-2019, the father re-partnered with Ms H and in 2021 the father and Ms H had a child.
In December 2021, the mother made allegations of sexual abuse by the father involving the children and on [...] December 2021 Territory Families intervened and the children were placed in the care of Ms H on the condition that she did not allow the father to be alone with the children.
On 17 February 2022, a protection order was made for the father to have responsibility for the children and the mother was permitted to spend time with them once per week. The mother spent time with Y but not X.
On 10 March 2022, X relocated to Queensland to live with the paternal grandmother.
In April 2022, the mother failed to return Y to the father's care as agreed. The mother attended upon a police station with Y and made allegations that the father had sexually abused Y. On 31 May 2022, interim orders were made providing for the children to live with the father and spend supervised time with the mother. In around this time, X returned to the Northern Territory to live with the father. Shortly thereafter, with the agreement of the father, X entered foster care and was transitioned between foster care placements.
On 7 August 2022, during a supervised visit at a contact centre, the mother (with the assistance of associates) removed Y from the contact centre and absconded with her to a rural location which was unknown to the father. X remained with the father. On 10 August 2022, a recovery order was made. On [...] August 2022, the mother presented with Y at a police station in Darwin and the child was returned to the father's care. The children have not spent face-to-face time with the mother since that incident.
On 25 November 2022, interim orders were made for supervised video calls between the mother and Y and on 1 December 2022 a protection order was made for the father to have responsibility for X for two years.
In November 2024, the mother was convicted of the abduction of Y and attempted abduction of X. She was sentenced in March 2025 to an intensive corrections order for two years subject to a home detention condition for the first 12 months
The father became aware that material was being published online regarding these proceedings and disparaging remarks about him. On 1 August 2025, orders were made restraining Ms Serene Teffaha from communicating or publishing information with respect to these proceedings. For the purposes of binding her to those orders, Ms Teffaha was joined to the proceedings for that application and not otherwise.
A number of Family Reports have been prepared in this matter and for the purposes of the latest Family Report, interviews were scheduled on 23 September 2025. The mother failed to attend those interviews. On 16 October 2025, Dr K released the Family Report which made the following recommendations on the basis of if the mother’s sentencing and current situation allows:
(a) forthwith, the mother spends supervised time with Y on a fortnightly basis commencing with one hour each occasion with increases gradually if no concerns with such time arises;

(b) otherwise, Y’s contact with the mother occurs as per the current arrangements and that consideration is given to weekly supervised phone contact; and

(c) X has contact with the mother, in a manner that is aligned with Y, at his discretion.

If the Court permits the father to relocate with the children, the following was recommended:
(a) Y spend supervised time with the mother for at least two of the four school holiday periods in Darwin and that such time comprise of at least three hours or as agreed and the mother be at liberty to organise to spend supervised time with Y in the father’s location during the other two school holiday periods on similar terms;

(b) otherwise, weekly supervised phone contact between Y and the mother is maintained and consideration is given to whether the father (or a nominated person) supervise such contact if feasible; and

(c) X has contact with the mother, in a manner that is aligned with Y, at his discretion.

It was said by Dr K that, in any case, the mother should:
(a) demonstrate sustained engagement in substantial, regular, and frequent psychological intervention, with the appointed treatment professional/s to have access to this Family Report and previous Family Reports; and

(b) complete further parental training by completing Circle of Security and Tuning into Teens programs as a minimum.

And, in any case, the mother:
(a) ensure that the children are protected from exposure to any beliefs that she may hold that may cause the children emotional harm;

(b) refrain from involving the children in any inappropriate discussion of court matters or expose the children in any form to the parental conflict, ill-feeling or negative views towards one another; and

(c) that any breach of these conditions would be considered a form of emotional harm and any contact or time spent between the mother and children should be suspended.

It was further recommended that the parties exclusively communicate via the Appclose co-parenting app (or share documents by email) exclusively.
The matter was listed for final hearing on 23 February 2026. Prior to the final hearing, there was correspondence with the legal representatives of the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) requesting that the parties have the capacity to appear remotely by way of MS Teams. The mother, both by herself and by her purported representative, Ms Teffaha, have corresponded with the Court. On the Friday prior to the commencement of the final hearing, Ms Teffaha copied the Court into correspondence sent by her, omitting irrelevant parts, to the effect that the mother requested the Attorney-General intervene in the final hearing and vacate the hearing pending the determination of a High Court matter she sought to initiate and that they were going to pursue remedies in other jurisdictions, namely the International Criminal Court, labelling these proceedings as a “kangaroo trial”. The mother did not attend the hearing either in person or by electronic means. In those circumstances, the Court found it appropriate to proceed on an undefended basis as against the mother.

PROPOSALS

By way of their Outline of Cases, the father and the ICL seek orders that the father have sole decision-making authority for major long-term issues in relation to the children, Y (and X, in accordance with his wishes) spend professionally supervised time with the mother on four occasions each year and, if it can be facilitated, supervised videos between the children and the mother (with X’s attendance subject to his wishes). The father also sought to relocate to Queensland, which was supported by the ICL.

EVIDENCE

For the purpose of the final hearing, the father relied upon the following documents:
(a) affidavit of Ms H filed 19 February 2026;

(b) affidavit of Mr Aberg filed 19 February 2026;

(c) Application in a Proceeding filed 19 February 2026;

(d) affidavit of Mr Aberg filed 24 January 2026;

(e) updated Family Report of Dr K filed 16 October 2025;

(f) addendum report of Dr K filed 31 August 2023;

(g) affidavit of Dr B annexing psychiatric assessment of the mother filed 27 April 2023;

(h) updated Family Report of Dr K filed 9 March 2023;

(i) Amended Initiating Application filed 23 September 2022;

(j) Notice of Risk filed 9 August 2022;

(k) Family Report of Ms J filed 30 November 2021;

(l) affidavit of Dr L annexing psychiatric assessment of the mother filed 4 August 2021; and

(m) Tender Bundle.

The ICL relied on the following:
(a) Family Report of Dr K filed 16 October 2025;

(b) addendum Family Report of Dr K filed 31 August 2025;

(c) affidavit of Dr B (psychiatric assessment of the mother) filed 27 April 2023;

(d) updated Family Report of Dr K 9 March 2023;

(e) Family Report of Ms J filed 30 November 2021;

(f) affidavit of Dr L (psychiatric assessment of the mother) filed 4 August 2021;

(g) info sharing – NT Police, Territory Families;

(h) documents produced under subpoena (Qld Police, NT Police, Department of Health);

(i) all orders made in these proceedings; and

(j) Tender Bundle.

The mother caused documents to be sent by way of correspondence (including an unsworn affidavit). As noted above, she did not attend the final hearing, and these documents were not filed.

RELEVANT LAW

In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to the children, I must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.[1]
Section 60CC(2) of the Act sets out the list of matters that the Court must consider in determining what is in the children's best interests.
Orders in respect of children are made under Part VII of the Act, where the meaning of a “parenting order” is defined (s 64B). The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D(1)), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B). The objects of Part VII of the Act are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child of 20 November 1989.[2] My decision is guided by those objects.
The Act confirms that safety includes safety from family violence, abuse, neglect or other harm.[3] Family violence and abuse are defined in the Act as “violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family or causes the family member to be fearful”.[4] Examples of such behaviour include assaults, repeated derogatory taunts, intentionally damaging property, unreasonably withholding financial support and preventing a family member from keeping connections with his or her family and friends.
The Explanatory Memorandum indicates further that “other harm” allows me to consider whether these children are affected by issues such as inter-parental conflict.[5]
“Safety” is not defined in the Act. I note that the Explanatory Memorandum to the Family Law Amendment Act 2023 states that s 60B(a) of the Act is “intended to make it clear to all users of the Family Law Act that safety is a fundamental consideration when considering the best interests of the child.”[6] It goes on to state that Courts should consider “what arrangements would encourage or support a removal, reduction or avoidance of harm”.[7]
In considering these matters I must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child; and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.[8]

Parental responsibility for children is vested in their parents (s 61C(1)), regardless of whether they live together or are separated (s 61C(2)), but that situation only applies whilst ever no order is made to change it (s 61C(3) and s 61D). Parental responsibility is defined to encompass all duties, powers, responsibilities and authority conferred by law upon parents (s 61B).
I am not required to resolve every controversy raised by the parties’ evidence or to refer to all the evidence raised by them. I refer to that evidence which is relevant to the issues that must be determined by the Court (Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48; (2003) 200 ALR 447 at [62]. See also Housing Commission (NSW) v Tatmar Pastoral Co Pty Ltd [1983] 3 NSWLR 378).
In Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 the Full Court held that the task of the Court in relation to questions of unacceptable risk is to undertake a qualitative analysis of the evidence directed not just to the existence of the risk of harm but also to the magnitude of the possible harm. An assessment of the probabilities is not the measure by which a future risk of harm is determined. A possibility may be sufficient basis upon which to find an unacceptable risk.

CONSIDERATION

Section 60CC(2)(a)

What arrangements would promote the safety of the child (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of the child and each person who has care of the child

Section 60CC(2A)

In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a) the Court must include consideration of any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child) and any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family

I agree with the submission made by the ICL that the children need to be protected from the mother’s views that they were both sexually abused by the father (and others). They also need to be protected from exposure to parental conflict to the greatest extent reasonably possible. Based on the mother's correspondence with the Court, her hostility towards the father remains unabated.
In relation to those allegations, it was urged on the Court to make a positive finding that the father had not sexually abused the children. In that regard, I refer to the sentencing remarks of Grant CJ at page 3 where his Honour stated:
However, you developed the belief that your son and daughter were being subjected to sexual abuse. Between [...] and [...] December 2022, you made a number of notifications to police and the Department of Children and Families to the effect that your daughter had disclosed to you that she had been sexually assaulted by her brother, her father, her father's new partner and the 12-year-old sister of her father's new partner. You also alleged that the father and his new partner had been sexually assaulting their 8-month-old daughter and that the father had been sexually assaulting his son.

Just to lay out the allegations in those terms illustrates their preposterous nature. Sadly, however, you appeared to believe them at the time, and it seems that you still believe them.
The mother has not sought to prove these allegations in court and I concur with the remarks of Grant CJ in relation to the allegations made by the mother against the father and his partner.[9]

Section 60CC(2)(b)

Any views expressed by the child

Dr K notes that X had some level of curiosity in relation to having contact with the mother but was generally ambivalent and uninformed about this. The Court was informed by the ICL that she was of the same view after meeting with X on 8 January 2026.
As to Y, the ICL advised the Court that she had indicated that she wanted face-to-face contact with the mother but was unable to respond as to a suggested frequency of ongoing video time. It is important to note that neither of the children have been made aware of the matters before the Court and they are not in a position to make an informed decision about their spend time arrangements with the mother.

Section 60CC(2)(c)

The developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child

As is apparent from the brief chronology, but made clearer by the affidavit evidence of the father, and the detailed account in the Family Report, X has experienced significant attachment disruption with both parents but is now settled in the father's care. The Court was advised that he is doing better in school and is well settled in the care of the father and Ms H. X has more significant needs as canvassed above.
Y has spent more time in contact with the mother, and the supervised video time has apparently worked well. The father is agreeable to continuing with supervised contact between Y and the mother and that is to the benefit of the child.

Section 60CC(2)(d)

The capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

The father has demonstrated the capacity to look after both the children since August 2022. He has done this in particularly difficult circumstances.
There are significant doubts regarding the mother's capacity to provide adequate and appropriate care for the children and to prioritise their needs. X has complex needs which are being met by the father and Ms H. The mother's failure to attend at the Family Report interviews reflects poorly on her and raises further doubts in relation to her capacity. The mother seems to be preoccupied with ongoing crusades against the father and the family law system generally which also impacts on her capacity.
The proposed relocation by the father, if permitted, will affect the ability of the mother to maintain supervised time, however, the orders proposed by the father allow face-to-face supervised time to continue. In that regard, his proposal for that to occur strongly indicates that he is child focused

Section 60CC(2)(e)

The benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so

There are real doubts as to whether X will obtain benefit from having an ongoing relationship with the mother at this time.
It is noted that the evidence of Dr K is to the effect that Y has continued benefit and is thriving in the father's care. He gives evidence, however, that Y has presented with a strong desire to continue her relationship with her mother. Notwithstanding, at paragraph 123 of the latest Family Report Dr K notes:
What informs [Y’s] perception of the mother must be taken into account when determining future contact or time spent together. In my opinion, [Y’s] perception of the mother - and also her desire to spend time with the mother – would be heavily based on how the mother presents and their interactions together in the confines of the supervised contact that they share – which of course may not accurately reflect how the mother may behave or interact with the child otherwise in her independent care.

Section 60CC(2)(f)

Anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the children

There is nothing further which is of relevance.

CONCLUSION

It was the recommendation of Dr K in his latest report that the children live with the father and that he should have sole parental responsibility. I agree with this.
It is apparent from the affidavit material filed by the father that the parties are unable to communicate in a cooperative and effective manner. The communications received by the Court from the mother indicate that she continues to hold trenchant views in relation to the father and the prospect of any cooperative relationship is very poor.
The father has cared for the children and the terms of the orders proposed by him make it plain that he wishes to facilitate a relationship between the children and the mother. It is appropriate in this case to allocate sole decision-making responsibility for major long-term issues to the father.
As noted above, the father sought orders that he be permitted to relocate with the children to Queensland. It is the case that there has been a high level of conflict between the parties and allegations in relation to the father continue to be placed online. The father has given evidence of the ongoing stressors associated with the allegations made against him and publicised. It is appropriate, and in the best interests of the children, having regard to the above, that he be permitted to relocate to Queensland with the children and Ms H (and their other children). While this may impact the mother’s capacity to spend time with the children, the orders provide her with the option to do so if she desires and I find that this is outweighed by the benefits to the children and the father in relocating. A relocation will be a means to remove the children from the conflict which has been present for so much of their lives and provide them, as well as the father, with a fresh start.
